text: Bisphenol A (BPA) is an endocrine disrupting chemical that is present in a vast number of everyday products. In light of a regulatory ban and a European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) decision to drastically decrease the acceptable daily intake value, the BPA substituting chemical family, comprising BPF, BPS and BPAF has been widely adopted. However, there is limited knowledge about the harmful effects of these substitutes on human health. This review delves into immunomodulatory effects of the emerging BPA analogues and their detrimental health outcomes through in silico, in vitro and in vivo studies. These studies demonstrate that all BPA analogues are capable of switching macrophages to a pro-inflammatory state, interfering with dendritic cell maturation and modulating T-cell cytokine production particularly Th17 cells. Furthermore, exposure to BPA analogues has also been associated with the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), genotoxicity on the genomic DNA of peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) and severe alterations in gut microbiota and intestinal immune homeostasis. This indicates that BPA analogues do not serve as as safe alternatives to BPA and that they can induce immunopathologies including atopy, allergic and autoimmune diseases. In conclusion, literature reviews of effects of BPA analogues are essential and there is much work that still needs to be done to establish effects of BPA analogues between sexes and mixture exposures of bisphenols.
